title: 2026 Lucid Gravity Touring review: A strong act 2
gist: Ars reviews the 2026 Lucid Gravity Touring ($82K base, $107,200 as tested), the lower-cost trim of Lucid's second model, finding it undercuts the $100K-plus Grand Touring by about $20,000 while retaining most of the charging speed, range, and design that define the Gravity, though interior material quality and fit-and-finish lag the price point.

insights.claim: The Gravity Touring uses a 16-module 89 kWh battery pack producing 560 hp and 811 lb-ft of torque with 337 miles of EPA range, starting at about $82,000, while the Grand Touring's 22-module 123 kWh pack delivers 828 hp and 407 miles of range starting above $100,000 - a roughly $20,000-plus spread for a step up in battery, power, and range within the same SUV platform.
insights.speaker: Jim Resnick
insights.so_what: Lucid is segmenting the Gravity SUV line by battery/motor tier rather than a new platform, a strategy that lets it hit a lower price point to chase volume without the capital cost of a distinct model.
insights.locator: Also fitted was Dream Drive 2.0 Pro

insights.claim: The reviewer argues the real-world performance and range gap between the Gravity Touring and the more expensive Grand Touring is 'less significant than the difference in stats columns,' implying the roughly $20,000 premium for the Grand Touring buys diminishing marginal returns for typical drivers.
insights.speaker: Jim Resnick
insights.so_what: If the lower trim satisfies most buyers' real-world needs, the Touring - not the Grand Touring - is more likely to be Lucid's volume driver, which matters for modeling Lucid's average selling price and margin mix.
insights.quote: I'd suggest the real-world performance difference between the top-power Grand Touring and the Touring is less significant than the difference in stats columns.

themes.theme: Charging speed as a competitive differentiator

insights.claim: The tested Gravity Touring charged from 15% to 95% state of charge in 31 minutes on a Tesla Supercharger via NACS with ISO 15118 plug-and-charge; Lucid claims the Gravity can add up to 200 miles of range in 11 minutes on a 400 kWh DC fast charger.
insights.speaker: Jim Resnick
insights.so_what: Lucid's charging speed and NACS/Supercharger network compatibility position it as competitive with or ahead of most EV rivals on a key purchase-decision factor for prospective buyers.
insights.locator: More importantly, the Gravity retains the charging performance

themes.theme: Execution gap between design and build quality

insights.claim: Despite the Gravity Touring's roughly six-figure as-tested price, the reviewer found several interior execution issues - including cheap-feeling removable cargo panels and floor mats that had already begun curling - that were reportedly worse than those in a $45,000 Subaru tested immediately afterward.
insights.speaker: Jim Resnick
insights.so_what: A quality-control and materials gap at this price point is a flag for Lucid's cost discipline as it pushes down-market to compete on price, relevant to warranty costs and brand perception as volume scales.

insights.claim: The Gravity Touring's cargo capacity (56 cubic feet behind the second row, expanding to 112 cubic feet with seats folded) exceeds the Ford Expedition's 108.5 cubic feet with all rows folded, positioning it competitively against large three-row combustion SUVs on utility, not just on EV range or performance.
insights.speaker: Jim Resnick
insights.so_what: Lucid is explicitly competing for large-SUV buyers on practical utility, broadening its addressable market beyond EV early adopters toward mainstream three-row SUV shoppers.
